text: When the radio announcer first took the job, finance was the last thing on his mind. He had been a careful, skillful young fellow with much courage but little money, and his only practicable plan was to please the public and hope for the best. Aboard a ferry one October morning, age eight in spirit if not in years, he had decided to become a voice that the nation would trust. "Necessarily," he told a fellow passenger, "I must speak accurately, or I am nothing." That resolution, laudable and firm, never wavered. Except for one absence caused by hepatitis, he had not missed a broadcast in eight years. His routine was strict: he would arrive early, list the headlines, and separately rehearse each summary aloud. He refused to be arrogant; arrogance, he believed, was an ugly streak that could drown a man's better self. Regarding the news itself, he tried to comprehend it deeply before he spoke. A story about official bribery, a hostage crisis, the prosecute of a snob who had tried to steal public funds, the monstrous evolution of a scandal at the aquarium board: each of these he treated with equal care. He would not let a triumphant tone creep in, nor would he smash a reputation lightly. One evening, a colleague tried to tantalize him with a rumor about a wholesaler who, it was said, would finance a corrupt campaign. "Alas," the announcer said, "I cannot air what I cannot prove. The disappearance of one fact can mislead a whole nation." He kept a small list of principles taped above his terminal, written in straight, careful lines. The software that ran the studio sometimes failed, and on those nights he would grope for a backup, refusing to drown the broadcast in dead air. He had studied biology and geometry at school, and a little of the temperate logic of those subjects stayed with him; he liked things that correspond to reason. When the petrol shortage struck and prices began to bob upward, he reported it without panic, separating real distribution problems from jumble and rumor. His lovely, refined manner made even bad news bearable. People said his voice could surround a frightened city like a refuge. He never sought to horrify, never let a howl of outrage into his tone. In time he was offered a promotion to the central desk, and though the suspense of the decision kept him awake, he accepted with a careful, indirectly grateful nod to his old mentor, the mister who had first taught him to speak. "You did not necessarily teach me facts," he wrote in a letter. "You taught me to arouse trust, and to keep it." He sealed the note, asked for a receipt, and posted it that very day. Years later, near a quiet tomb on a hill, he would recline and recall that the whole long enterprise had begun with one boy's stubborn refusal to lie. He refused to hound a source piecemeal; instead he would enquire closely, decipher every decimal of a budget, and seek the concurrence of two editors before air. Low morale could affect a whole newsroom, so he fed his team good nourishment and humor to prevent any relapse into cynicism. A touch of refinement marked his prose; he would never suspend his standards. He loved the scent of fresh ink. After a reception one night, he wrote a caption for a photo of his fiance beside a wall of white gypsum, lit by soft light. From the occidental desk to a long trek across the country, he cast aside the fear of failure. 'Ditto,' his editor would say to every careful choice, and the young man took flight under his mentor's wing.
translation: 当这位电台播音员第一次接下这份工作时，财务是他最不在意的事。他曾是个细心、娴熟的年轻人，有的是勇气，钱却不多，他唯一可行的计划就是取悦公众，并寄望于最好的结果。在十月一个清晨的渡轮上，即使年龄不到、心境却如八岁孩童般，他已决定要成为一个全国都信任的声音。"必然地，"他对一位同船旅客说，"我必须准确地说话，否则我什么都不是。"那个值得称赞而坚定的决心，从未动摇。除了一次因肝炎而缺席之外，八年来他没有错过一次播报。他的惯例很严格：他会早早到达，列出头条，并分别把每段摘要大声排练一遍。他拒绝傲慢；他相信傲慢是一道丑陋的纹路，会淹没一个人更好的自我。至于新闻本身，他在开口前力求深刻地理解它。一桩官员受贿的报道、一场人质危机、对一个曾试图窃取公款的势利小人的起诉、水族馆董事会一桩丑闻可怕的演变：他对每一件都同等谨慎对待。他不会让得意洋洋的腔调溜进来，也不会轻率地砸毁谁的名声。一天傍晚，一位同事想用一则传闻逗弄他，说某个批发商据传会资助一场腐败的竞选。"唉，"播音员说，"我无法播出我无法证实的东西。一个事实的消失就能误导整个国家。"他在终端机上方贴着一小张原则清单，用笔直、工整的字迹写成。运行演播室的软件有时会出故障，那些夜里他会摸索备用方案，拒绝让广播淹没在静默之中。他在学校学过生物学和几何学，那些科目中温和的逻辑有一点留在了他身上；他喜欢符合理性的东西。当汽油短缺来袭、价格开始上下浮动时，他报道此事毫不惊慌，把真正的分配问题与杂乱的谣言区分开来。他可爱而优雅的举止让坏消息都变得可以承受。人们说他的声音能像避难所一样环绕一座受惊的城市。他从不想制造恐惧，从不让一声愤怒的嚎叫进入他的语调。后来他获得晋升到中央台的机会，尽管这个决定的悬念让他彻夜难眠，他还是接受了，向他的老导师——那位最初教他说话的先生——含蓄而感激地点了点头。"你未必教了我事实，"他在一封信里写道，"你教我去唤起信任，并守住它。"他封好便笺，索取一张收据，当天就寄了出去。多年以后，在一座山上安静的坟墓旁，他会斜倚下来，回想这整桩漫长的事业，竟始于一个男孩固执地拒绝撒谎。他拒绝零敲碎打地追逼一个消息来源；相反，他会仔细打听，破译预算的每一个小数，并在播出前寻求两位编辑的一致同意。低落的士气会影响整个新闻编辑室，于是他给团队提供良好的滋养和幽默，防止任何重新陷入愤世嫉俗的旧病复发。他的文字带着一丝优雅；他绝不会暂停自己的标准。他喜欢新鲜油墨的气味。一天夜里一场招待会之后，他为一张照片写了说明文字，照片里他的未婚妻站在一面白色石膏墙旁，被柔和的灯光照着。从西方编辑台到一次横穿全国的长途跋涉，他抛开了对失败的恐惧。'同上，'他的编辑会对每一个谨慎的选择这样说，而这位年轻人在导师的羽翼下展翅高飞。
